story.headline: Spain are world champions — Torres' extra-time strike beats Argentina 1-0
story.summary: Ferran Torres' extra-time goal gave Spain a 1-0 win over defending champions Argentina in the 2026 World Cup final, Spain's second title and a European-plus-world double.
story.display_body: Ferran Torres, on as a substitute, drove home early in extra time (around the 106th minute) to give Spain a 1-0 win over Argentina in the World Cup final at MetLife Stadium outside New York on Sunday. It is Spain's second world title after 2010, and it completes a rare double: they were already European champions, so one nation now holds both trophies at once. The result crowns a young Spanish side built around Lamine Yamal and Nico Williams as the dominant force in the men's game — and it closes the book on Argentina's title defence, and on the 39-year-old Lionel Messi's bid to leave with the trophy in hand. The holders were reduced to ten men (Enzo Fernandez sent off) and managed only two shots across 120 minutes; Spain conceded just one goal in the whole tournament, the meanest defence of any World Cup winner, over a record eight matches in the new 48-team format co-hosted by the USA, Canada and Mexico. Torres became only the second substitute ever to score a World Cup final winner.
story.why: This is the sport's biggest prize, and it settles the argument over who the team of the era is — not Argentina, France or Brazil, but a Spain side young enough to stay there. For a casual viewer, it's the tournament that will frame the next four years of football.

story.headline: Switzerland's Berset tells FIFA to fix the game's integrity before 2030
story.summary: Council of Europe chief Alain Berset demanded FIFA build an integrity framework before 2030, citing a red card he attributed to a Trump phone call, racist abuse and betting expansion.
story.display_body: As the final whistle blew, Alain Berset — the former Swiss federal councillor now Secretary General of the Council of Europe (the 46-nation human-rights body based in Strasbourg, separate from the EU) — publicly pressed FIFA to open an "integrity" dialogue that same evening and to build a framework before the 2030 World Cup. His central example, per SRF's reporting: a red card against US forward Folarin Balogun that was rescinded, which Berset attributed to a phone call from President Donald Trump — political influence, he argued, reaching onto the pitch itself. He also flagged racist abuse of players and FIFA's expansion into stadium betting partnerships as openings for fraud. The framing here — 'integrity', and the causal link between Trump's call and the reversal — is Berset's and SRF's characterisation; FIFA has not formally responded, and the causation claim rests on a single source.
story.why: When a European human-rights institution publicly asks whether a head of state swayed a match decision, the story stops being about the scoreline and becomes about who actually controls the game heading into the next tournament.

story.headline: Antonelli wins at Spa and opens a commanding 45-point title lead
story.summary: Kimi Antonelli won the Belgian GP ahead of Leclerc and Verstappen; with rival Russell crashing out, his F1 title lead over Hamilton grew to 45 points.
story.display_body: Kimi Antonelli, the 19-year-old Mercedes driver, won the Belgian Grand Prix by 1.9 seconds from Ferrari's Charles Leclerc, with four-time champion Max Verstappen third — his sixth win in ten races this season. The result that matters, though, was behind him: his nearest challenger and Mercedes team-mate George Russell crashed out on the opening lap after contact with Lewis Hamilton, who took a contested five-second penalty and finished fourth. With Russell scoring nothing, Antonelli now leads the drivers' championship by 45 points over second-placed Hamilton, with Russell a further five points back in third. A weekend that could have tightened the title fight instead blew it open — and left a teenager, rather than the established champions, in clear control.
story.why: F1's next generation isn't coming, it's here: Antonelli is on course to be one of the youngest champions in the sport's history, and with ten races run the veterans are now effectively racing each other for second.

story.headline: Evenepoel wins in the Alps — but Vingegaard's crash hands the Tour to Pogacar
story.summary: Remco Evenepoel won Tour stage 15 while Jonas Vingegaard abandoned with a broken collarbone, leaving Tadej Pogacar leading the general classification by about five minutes.
story.display_body: On the Tour's first serious mountain day — Stage 15, Champagnole to the Plateau de Solaison in the Alps — Belgium's Remco Evenepoel outsprinted overall leader Tadej Pogacar for the stage win. But the day turned on a crash: Jonas Vingegaard, the two-time Tour champion and the one rider realistically able to challenge Pogacar, went down roughly 20 km from the finish, broke his collarbone and abandoned the race. That leaves Pogacar leading the general classification (GC — the overall time standings that decide the winner) by about five minutes from Evenepoel, with Isaac del Toro third at 5:58. In effect, the only man who has ever beaten Pogacar at the Tour is out, all but confirming the Slovenian's expected fifth title with the race's final week still to come.
story.why: A three-week Grand Tour is decided as much by who survives as by who is fastest; a collarbone, not a rival's legs, has drained most of the suspense from the finish.

story.headline: Staudenmann wins the Rigi-Schwinget as the Swiss wrestling season builds
story.summary: Bernese wrestler Fabian Staudenmann beat Lars Zaugg in the final bout to win the Rigi-Schwinget, his second win there after 2024 and his 23rd festival crown.
story.display_body: Bernese wrestler Fabian Staudenmann won the Rigi-Schwinget — one of the summer's mountain festivals in Schwingen (Swiss wrestling, contested in a sawdust ring), held on the Rigi above Lake Lucerne — beating fellow Bernese Lars Zaugg in the final bout (Schlussgang) on Sunday. It is his second win on the Rigi after 2024 and his 23rd festival crown. With no Eidgenossisches (the sport's championship, staged only every three years) on the 2026 calendar, individual festival wins like this are how the pecking order among the top wrestlers gets set through late summer.
story.why: For a Swiss reader, Schwingen is a genuine national event, not a niche; Staudenmann is quietly building the form that counts when the biggest festivals arrive.

story.headline: US strikes Iran for ninth consecutive night as third US soldier killed and Hormuz fight escalates
story.summary: The US carried out a ninth night of air strikes on Iran on 20 July amid a collapsing ceasefire and a fight for the Strait of Hormuz; a third US service member was killed, Iran retaliated on Gulf-state bases, and Brent crude rose above $90.
story.display_body: The US struck Iran for a ninth consecutive night early Monday (20 July) as a month-old ceasefire kept unravelling and the fight for the Strait of Hormuz widened. Iranian state media (IRIB, Fars) reported explosions from the Gulf ports of Bandar Mahshahr and Bandar Imam Khomeini to Jask, Sirik, Konarak and Chabahar on the southern coast, and — for the first time in this escalation — as far north as Tabriz. The US military said it hit command centres, air defences, coastal-surveillance and missile/drone launch sites to degrade Iran's ability to attack shipping; Iran's Revolutionary Guard (IRGC) said it fired missiles and drones from Lorestan at US assets, claiming strikes on airbases in Jordan (Aqaba, Azraq) and Kuwait (Ali Al Salem), with sirens also sounding in Bahrain. The US now confirms a third service member killed in recent days in Jordan and Iraq; President Trump said the latest strikes were carried out "in honour" of those troops and repeated that "Iran cannot have a nuclear weapon." Washington says it is enforcing a naval blockade of Iranian ports while the IRGC says two oil tankers "exploded" overnight attempting what it called an "unsafe" corridor through the strait. Secretary of State Marco Rubio, speaking in the Philippines, said the US remained "open to diplomacy" but "it has to be real," urging other countries to help protect Hormuz shipping. Brent crude rose above $90 a barrel for the first time in over a month — a direct cost for import-dependent Switzerland.
story.why: A great-power air campaign, mounting US casualties and a contested chokepoint that carries a fifth of the world's seaborne oil is the combination most likely to turn a regional war into a global economic shock.

story.headline: Andy Burnham becomes UK prime minister, replacing Keir Starmer
story.summary: Andy Burnham took office as UK prime minister on 20 July after Keir Starmer resigned, pledging a 10-year plan, cost-of-living action and scrapping the digital-ID scheme.
story.display_body: Andy Burnham took office as UK prime minister on Monday (20 July), completing Labour's mid-term handover after Keir Starmer resigned. The 56-year-old mayor of Greater Manchester — a lifelong Labour member with no seat in the current Parliament — was named party leader on 17 July through an internal process in which no rival stood, and received King Charles III's authority to form a government at Buckingham Palace on Monday before entering No 10. He is the UK's fifth prime minister in four years (seventh since 2010). Burnham promised a "10-year plan," "early moves" on the cost of living, and a "reset of priorities" whose first concrete step is scrapping Starmer's digital-ID scheme; prisons minister James (Lord) Timpson resigned as the reshuffle began. He has signalled a "change of emphasis" toward the North Sea oil-and-gas industry — enough for Trump to claim on social media, disputed by Burnham's allies, that Britain would open the basin "all the way."
story.why: Britain gets its seventh leader in a decade amid a rising Reform UK, and Burnham's instincts — devolution, renationalising rail and water, a warmer line on Europe — could reshape UK–EU relations that bear directly on Switzerland's own bilateral track.

story.headline: DRC Ebola outbreak nears 1,000 deaths as Canada imposes entry ban
story.summary: An Ebola outbreak in eastern DR Congo has killed close to 1,000 of 2,344 confirmed cases; Canada barred entry to recent travellers on 20 July, against WHO advice.
story.display_body: The Ebola outbreak in eastern DR Congo has killed close to 1,000 people out of 2,344 confirmed cases, and Canada barred entry to recent travellers on Monday (20 July). More than one in three confirmed infections has been fatal in what authorities call the fastest-spreading Ebola outbreak on record, which began about two months ago in the country's east; the death toll has jumped from roughly 600 reported on 9 July. Canada's health agency said its ban — blocking non-residents who were in the DRC within the past 21 days — took effect Monday, following a similar US restriction last week. Both go against WHO guidance, which rates the risk of international spread as low and advises against travel or trade restrictions on the grounds they stigmatise affected countries and hamper epidemic control.
story.why: A lethal, fast-moving outbreak colliding with unilateral border closures reopens the hard pandemic-era question of whether such bans protect anyone or mainly punish the countries doing the containing.

story.headline: Aargau prosecutor seeks life sentence for ex-SVP MP Patrick Frei
story.summary: The Aargau public prosecutor on 20 July demanded life imprisonment for former SVP cantonal MP Patrick Frei, accused of drugging and sexually assaulting several women and a minor and of attempted murder.
story.display_body: The Aargau public prosecutor on Monday (20 July) demanded a life sentence for former SVP cantonal MP Patrick Frei, accused of drugging and sexually assaulting several women and a minor. Frei, 57, a former member of the Aargau Grand Council held in pretrial detention, faces charges that include multiple counts of attempted murder, aggravated rape and sexual acts with children; prosecutors allege he rendered victims unconscious with knock-out drops before assaulting them. The case came to light in September 2023 after the mother of one victim caught him in the act.
story.why: A demand for Switzerland's most severe penalty against a former elected official makes this one of the gravest political-figure criminal cases the canton has seen, and the verdict will test how the courts weigh the attempted-murder framing built on the alleged drugging.

story.headline: Fire guts attic of Lucerne hotel; 60 evacuated
story.summary: A Sunday-evening fire in the attic of the Hotel Continental Park near Lucerne station forced about 60 evacuations and lightly injured one firefighter.
story.display_body: A fire gutted the attic of the Hotel Continental Park near Lucerne's railway station on Sunday evening (19 July), forcing about 60 evacuations. The alarm was raised at 19:39 and the blaze — which destroyed several attic rooms — was under control by around 22:00, Lucerne city fire services said. One firefighter was lightly injured in the arm and taken to hospital; six other people and two firefighters were checked for possible smoke inhalation, and guests and staff were evacuated in time. Around 120 responders from several fire corps were mobilised, and crews prevented the flames from spreading to neighbouring buildings.

reason: See, you said "final" night and the US just continued. You need to assume an unreliable narrator from the trump administration, it's not politics, it's just facts, they lie all the time. Maybe we need to add reminders of the source reliability in the prompts?
